additives.name: acetic acid
additives.role: acid additive to enhance reaction rate

mechanistic_details.key_intermediate: bromine radicals from homolysis of trace Br2 in NBS

chemistry.scope_summary: Applied to 9 different (hetero)aromatic compounds including substrates with boronic ester, ketone, pyrimidine, heterocyclic cores, esters, and nitro groups. Residence times ranged from 1.36-18 minutes in flow with good to excellent conversions.

optimization_table.key_findings: MeCN/AcOH (1:1) solvent system, 405 nm LEDs at 200 mA current, 0.1 M concentration, 1.5 eq NBS loading

figures.caption: Scheme 1.  The benzylic bromination of  1 , studied in this report, under photochemical conditions. | Figure 1.  UV/Vis absorption spectroscopy ( l = 300–750 nm) of the reaction components in the benzylic bromination of 4-methyl-3-(trifluoromethyl) benzonitrile ( 1 ).

Figure 2.  a) The effect of light intensity upon rate of reaction showing starting material consumption over time, for the benzylic bromination of  1 at different light intensities. b) Calibration of luminous flux with changing current.

figures.trend: yield increases from 4.5 to 5 min, then remains relatively stable
figures.caption: Figure 4.  Product distribution of benzylic bromination of  1  in flow, using different residence times, to determine the optimal flow rate.

figures.trend: Yield increases with current up to ~250-300 mA, then plateaus or slightly decreases at higher currents. Lower flow rates (3 mL/min) generally give higher yields than higher flow rates.
figures.caption: Figure 6.  a) Reaction scheme for the large scale bromination of ester 15 to form its benzylic bromide 9. b) Chart showing the solution yield of reaction at different flow rates and light intensities.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Long reaction times (240 minutes) and thermal heating required using CCl4 reflux conditions
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Dramatic time reduction to 2.5 minutes residence time with LED irradiation at controlled temperature (30°C), enabling reliable temperature control and lower energy consumption
translation_logic.what_changed: Heating method changed from thermal reflux to LED photochemical activation at 405 nm, residence time reduced from 240 to 2.5 minutes
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Same substrate concentration (0.1 M), same solvent system (MeCN/AcOH), same stoichiometry range
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Elimination of high temperature reflux conditions and use of CCl4 solvent

figure_descriptions: Mechanistic scheme showing benzylic bromination reaction
Residence time optimization for flow conditions
Large-scale flow rate and light intensity optimization

conflicts: Figure 4 shows optimal residence times of both 2.5 min and 5 min depending on NBS loading, but flow_optimized uses 2.5 min value
notes: Well-documented photochemical flow process with clear optimization data. Missing some quantitative batch baseline data (yield, exact conditions). Throughput calculated from 1 mmol scale and 2.5 min residence time. Multiple optimization figures provide good validation of conditions.
